---
title: "Research"
---

::: {.section}
::: {.section-title}
## Research programme

My research sits at the intersection of health economics, stated preference methods, behavioural science, and applied policy evaluation.
:::

::: {.grid-2}
::: {.card}
### 1. Health preference research
Discrete choice experiments and stated preference methods applied to organ transplantation, diabetes, cancer, dementia, loneliness, telehealth, medical devices, vaccines, and health workforce questions.
:::
::: {.card}
### 2. Information processing in choice
Eye tracking, attribute attention, cost-information processing, attribute ordering, numeracy, feedback, and how respondents simplify or structure multi-attribute choices.
:::
::: {.card}
### 3. Vaccine policy and public acceptability
Public preferences for vaccine characteristics, mandates, exemptions, social restrictions, and future pandemic policy design across international settings.
:::
::: {.card}
### 4. Equity, value and policy design
Preference-based value assessment, equity–efficiency trade-offs, worse-off priority, and design of policy instruments that reflect public and patient values.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.section}
::: {.section-title}
## Fields

:::
<span class="tag">Health economics</span>
<span class="tag">Behavioural economics</span>
<span class="tag">Discrete choice experiments</span>
<span class="tag">Eye tracking</span>
<span class="tag">Clinical outcomes assessment</span>
<span class="tag">Health technology assessment</span>
<span class="tag">Vaccine policy</span>
<span class="tag">Decision support tools</span>
:::

::: {.callout}
The University of Newcastle profile lists health economics and behavioural economics as formal fields of research, with published outputs in journals including <em>Journal of Health Economics</em>, <em>Health Economics</em>, <em>Social Science & Medicine</em>, <em>Empirical Economics</em>, <em>Health Policy</em>, <em>Value in Health</em>, <em>npj Vaccines</em>, <em>BMJ Open</em>, and <em>The Bone & Joint Journal</em>.
:::
